detectgxt 0.0.0.9000
  • Get started
  • Reference

    DetectGxT: detecting gene-by-treatment interactions on molecular count phenotypes accounting for allelic additivity

    Yuriko Harigaya, Michael Love, William Valdar

    2026-07-04

    detectgxt.Rmd

    Introduction

    The DetectGxT software provides functions for detecting gene-by-treatment (GxT) interactions on molecular count phenotypes, such as RNA-seq and ATAC-seq data. It aims to increase the statistical power to detect interaction (response) molecular QTLs using a nonlinear regression model that captures the relationship between genotypes and transformed molecular count phenotypes more accurately than a commonly used linear regression model.

    This software is primarily designed for performing interaction QTL mapping focusing on a set of candidate pairs of features (e.g., gene expression and ATAC-peak regions) and SNPs for which significant genotype effects have been identified in at least one of the control and treatment conditions. Note that this type of multi-step approach has been adopted in previous studies, such as Alasoo et al. (2018) and Matoba et al. (2024).

    A typical data analysis procedure using this package is as follows:

    • Step 1: condition-stratified molecular QTL mapping using linear regression
    • Step 2: interaction molecular QTL mapping using nonlinear regression
      • obtaining the union set from condition-stratified molecular QTL mapping
      • preparing genotype data using get_geno() (optional)
      • preparing phenotype data using preprocess_pheno() (optional)
      • performing interaction QTL mapping using map_qtl()

    Minimally, the input for the main function map_qtl() is genotypes, pre-processed phenotypes, sequencing sample annotations, and candidate feature-SNP pairs. See Input data for details. Optionally, covariate information and a kinship matrix may be supplied. The output is a data frame containing a row per feature-SNP pair, with estimated coefficients and nominal PP values for non-zero GxT interaction.

    Quick start

    We first show the minimal code to run DetectGxT. See Input data for how to format the input.

    # perform interaction QTL mapping
res <- map_qtl(
    candidate=candidate, fn="nonlinear",
    anno=anno, geno=geno, pheno=pheno)

    Input data

    Required

    The minimal input for the main function map_qtl() is data frames containing candidate feature-SNP pairs, genotypes, processed molecular count phenotypes, and sample annotations. The formats of the geno and pheno data frames are the same as those for MatrixEQTL.

    • candidate: A data frame containing feature IDs and SNP IDs in the first and second columns, respectively.
    • geno: A data frame formatted as input for MatrixEQTL. The first column must contain SNP IDs. The rest of the columns must contain genotypes, which can be allelic dosage or integer-coded genotypes (0, 1, 2). Each row corresponds to each SNP. Each column corresponds to each sample.
    • pheno: A data frame formatted as input for MatrixEQTL. The first column must contain feature IDs (e.g., genes and ATAC-peak regions). The rest of the columns must contain pre-processed molecular phenotypes. Each row corresponds to each feature. Each column corresponds to each sample.
    • anno: A data frame containing sequencing sample IDs, subject IDs, and treatment conditions (0 or 1) in the first, second, and third columns, respectively. The columns must be named as “sample”, “subject”, and “condition”.

    Optional

    For the minimal code in Quick start, we assume that the confounding factors have already been regressed out of the phenotypes. We provide options to account for confounding factors through the following input arguments to the map_qtl() function.

    • covar: A data frame containing fixed effect covariates. The first column must contain sample IDs. The rest of the columns must contain covariate values. Each row corresponds to each sample. Each column corresponds to each covariate.
    • kinship: A matrix representing kinship (polygenic structure). The row and column names of the matrix must correspond to the set of unique elements in the subject column in anno.

    The format of the covar data frame is the same as that for MatrixEQTL.

    Loading packages 

    library(detectgxt)

    Step 1: Condition-stratified molecular QTL mapping using linear regression

    In this vignette, we consider interaction (response) molecular QTL mapping on a pre-selected set of feature-SNP pairs obtained by condition-stratified molecular QTL mapping. Condition-stratified molecular QTL mapping can be performed based on linear regression or linear mixed-effect models using standard software packages, such as MatrixEQTL, TensorQTL, and limix_qtl. This step includes appropriate multiple testing correction and SNP filtering by selecting lead SNPs, performing LD clumping, or conducting conditional analysis. See Alasoo et al. (2018) and Matoba et al. (2024) for details.

    Although assuming linearity between genotypes and transformed molecular count phenotypes can lead to inaccurate effect size estimation, this is unlikely to be a significant issue in detecting associations in a single condition. Therefore, we recommend using the standard linear regression approach in step 1 for computational efficiency.

    Step 2: Interaction (response) molecular QTL mapping using nonlinear regression

    Obtaining the union set from step 1

    It is necessary to obtain the union set of feature-SNP pairs from step 1 and create a data frame that can be passed to map_qtl() using the candidate input argument. We do not provide a function to perform this task, as it depends on upstream tools used in step 1. See Input data for the format of the data frame.

    Formatting sample annotations

    In this section, we generate the anno data frame, which is used throughout the remaining examples. See Input data for the format of the data frame. For simplicity, we assume a fully paired design, although the package also supports unpaired and partially paired data.

    n.sample <- 160 # number of sequencing samples
n.sub <- 80 # number of subjects

anno <- data.frame(
    sample=paste0("s", seq_len(n.sample)),
    subject=paste0("d", rep(seq_len(n.sub), each=2)),
    condition=rep(c(0, 1), times=n.sub))

head(anno)
#>   sample subject condition
#> 1     s1      d1         0
#> 2     s2      d1         1
#> 3     s3      d2         0
#> 4     s4      d2         1
#> 5     s5      d3         0
#> 6     s6      d3         1

    Preparing genotype data

    In this section, we demonstrate how to process genotype data in the PLINK format to generate a geno data frame, using get_geno(), which can be passed to map_qtl() using the geno argument. Users can pre-process the data using alternative methods, if preferred.

    The get_geno() function takes four arguments:

    • candidate: See Input data.
    • chromosomes: An integer vector of chromosome numbers to search for the candidate SNPs.
    • plink.prefix: The path prefix shared by all PLINK files, up to and including the base.
    • plink.suffix: The file extension of the PLINK binary files (e.g., "bed").

    PLINK files must follow the naming convention <plink.prefix>.<chromosome>.<plink.suffix>, with .bim and .fam files sharing the same base name. For example, with plink.prefix="path/to/geno", chromosomes=c(1, 2), and plink.suffix="bed", the function reads path/to/geno.1.bed and path/to/geno.2.bed (along with the corresponding .bim and .fam files). SNPs from all requested chromosomes are stacked into a single output data frame.

    The following example uses the PLINK files bundled with the package.

    plink.prefix <- file.path(
    system.file("extdata", "plink", package="detecther"), "geno")

candidate.geno <- data.frame(
    feat_id=c("f1", "f2"),
    snp_id=c("g1", "g3"))

geno.ex <- get_geno(
    candidate=candidate.geno,
    chromosomes=1,
    plink.prefix=plink.prefix,
    plink.suffix="bed")

head(geno.ex)
#>   snp_id d1 d2 d3 d4 d5 d6
#> 1     g1  0  0  1  1  2  2
#> 2     g3  0  0  1  1  2  2

    Preparing phenotype data

    In this section, we demonstrate how to pre-process raw molecular count data to generate a pheno data frame, using preprocess_pheno(), which can be passed to map_qtl() using the pheno argument. Users can pre-process the data using alternative methods, if preferred.

    In the following section, we generate example count data (count) using the make_count_data() function. Users with existing molecular count data may skip to Input to preprocess_pheno().

    Simulating molecular count data

    Click to expand

    To create molecular count data, we first specify the number of features.

    n.feat <- 10000 # number of features (e.g., genes, ATAC-peaks)

    We then use make_count_data(), which simulates negative-binomial count data using the nonlinear GxT parametrization consistent with the models in map_qtl(). By default, all effects are set to zero via sd=c(0, 0, 0). To introduce genotype, treatment, or interaction effects into the simulated counts, specify sd (to draw per-feature effects from Normal(0, sd[k]), where k indexes genotype, treatment, and interaction) or coef (to fix effects at a given value); exactly one of sd[k] or coef[k] must be non-NA for each effect.

    count <- make_count_data(
    anno=anno, n.feat=n.feat, n.sample=n.sample)

    Input to preprocess_pheno()

    The following code displays the count data generated in the previous section.

    count[1:5, 1:5]
#>     s1  s2  s3  s4  s5
#> f1   4   3  12   0  15
#> f2  60 126 173  76  63
#> f3   0   0   0   0   0
#> f4  57 106  66  32  76
#> f5 365 253 225 119 143

    Running preprocess_pheno()

    The preprocess_pheno() function first scales and transforms the data. Then it residualizes the data against the treatment indicators, performs principal component analysis, and residualizes the transformed data against a desired number of principal components.

    The preprocess_pheno() function takes arguments including:

    • count: A matrix containing molecular counts (non-negative integers). The row and column names correspond to feature IDs and sample IDs, respectively.
    • anno: See Input data.
    • num.pc: An integer specifying the number of molecular phenotype principal components to control for.

    The following code runs the function using the anno and count objects generated above.

    pheno <- preprocess_pheno(
    count=count, anno=anno, num.pc=10)

pheno[1:5, 1:5]
#>   feat_id          s1         s2         s3          s4
#> 1      f1  0.52375774  0.1677593  0.5179037 -1.43289457
#> 2      f2 -0.41880813  0.1979051  0.6057498 -0.03943474
#> 3      f3 -0.33996767 -0.2226068 -0.2359433 -0.39787193
#> 4      f4  0.07208729  0.7853798  0.2156213 -0.37325833
#> 5      f5  0.72546085  0.4999999  0.1621861 -0.41803018

    Note that the pheno object above is only for demonstrating the usage of preprocess_pheno() and will not be used in the subsequent section, as it does not include genotype or treatment effects.

    The number of principal components can be adjusted as described in Matoba et al. (2024). Alternatively, a scree plot or an automatic elbow detection method can be used to select an appropriate number. In the latter case, the list argument can be set to TRUE to obtain the output from the prcomp() function from the stats package.

    pheno <- preprocess_pheno(
    count=count, anno=anno, num.pc=10, list=TRUE)

names(pheno)
#> [1] "pheno" "pca"

    In this case, the output is a list object containing the pheno data frame and the pca object returned from prcomp().

    Interaction QTL mapping using map_qtl()

    In this section, we demonstrate how to use map_qtl(). The function takes candidate, geno, pheno, and anno as required input and returns a data frame containing effect sizes and PP values.

    In the following section, we generate example input data (candidate, geno, pheno) using the make_sim_data() function. Users with existing pre-processed phenotype data may skip to Input to map_qtl().

    Generating data using make_sim_data()

    Click to expand

    In this section, we generate genotype and phenotype data using the make_sim_data() function, which will be used for demonstrating the usage of the map_qtl() function. Note that make_sim_data() returns simulated phenotype data that mimics preprocessed data.

    We will draw genotype, treatment, and interaction effect sizes from Normal distributions with zero mean and effect-specific standard deviations. The following code specifies the standard deviations.

    sd <- c(1.5, 2.0, 1.0) # genotype, treatment, interaction

    We will generate data based on eight models corresponding to all 232^3 combinations of the genotype, treatment, and interaction effects being either zero or non-zero. Specifically, we define models 1-8 as follows.

    Model Genotype Treatment Interaction
    1 0 0 0
    2 1 0 0
    3 0 1 0
    4 1 1 0
    5 0 0 1
    6 1 0 1
    7 0 1 1
    8 1 1 1

    Here, 0 and 1 indicate that the effect is zero and non-zero, respectively. The number of replicates per model can be specified using the num input argument, which expects an eight-dimensional integer vector. With the following vector, 900 simulations will be based on the fourth model, and 100 simulations will be based on the eighth model.

    num <- c(0, 0, 0, 900, 0, 0, 0, 100)

    The following code generates data for 1000 feature-SNP pairs.

    d <- make_sim_data(
    num=num, anno=anno, fn="nonlinear", sd=sd, filter.geno=TRUE)

    Note that, if filter.geno is set to TRUE, SNPs for which either of the homozygotes is missing in at least one of the control and treated conditions are excluded. See Running map_qtl() for details.

    Input to map_qtl()

    The following code displays the input data generated in the previous section. Note that only the first two columns are required in the candidate data frame, while this example contains additional columns.

    head(d$candidate) # candidate
#>   feat_id snp_id model       maf b0         b1         b2 b3
#> 1      f1     g1     4 0.1694789  0  0.8411191 -0.9055679  0
#> 2      f2     g2     4 0.3756608  0 -1.3180614  2.3259291  0
#> 3      f3     g3     4 0.2091136  0 -2.2617105 -4.4458923  0
#> 4      f4     g4     4 0.4745412  0 -0.4854759 -0.5104962  0
#> 5      f5     g5     4 0.3371473  0  0.7503507 -2.3098373  0
#> 6      f6     g6     4 0.3786667  0  3.6018332  1.6039236  0

d$geno[1:5, 1:5] # geno
#>   snp_id d1 d2 d3 d4
#> 1     g1  0  0  1  0
#> 2     g2  0  1  0  2
#> 3     g3  0  0  2  0
#> 4     g4  2  0  1  0
#> 5     g5  1  1  0  1

d$pheno[1:5, 1:5] # pheno
#>   feat_id          s1         s2         s3        s4
#> 1      f1 -0.83204330 -2.0721385 -1.0655906 -2.469350
#> 2      f2 -2.00016494  1.7811384 -0.8796291  1.535850
#> 3      f3 -1.17867244 -6.2286525 -0.9879191 -3.717404
#> 4      f4 -0.91003067 -2.3049397  1.8297305 -1.940412
#> 5      f5  0.08363835 -0.9590932  0.4192116 -2.129436

    Running map_qtl()

    We now perform interaction QTL mapping using map_qtl(). See Required for the required input arguments, candidate, geno, pheno, and anno. As the phenotype is assumed to have been residualized against covariates, we omit the covar input argument (see Optional). We omit kinship, as we do not include random effects in this example. See Including random effects for how to include random effects.

    Additional arguments include:

    • fn: A character string specifying the function. This must be one of “nonlinear” and “linear”, corresponding to nonlinear and linear models, respectively.
    • filter.geno: A Boolean variable as to whether to ensure that homozygotes for both alleles have at least one observation in both control and treated conditions.
    res <- map_qtl(
    candidate=d$candidate, fn="nonlinear",
    geno=d$geno, pheno=d$pheno, anno=anno,
    filter.geno=TRUE)

head(res)
#>   feat_id snp_id       beta0     beta_g     beta_t   beta_gxt     sigma sigma_u
#> 1      f1     g1 -0.08506611  0.9250519 -1.0026361  0.1488315 0.9223795      NA
#> 2      f2     g2  0.15370802 -1.3154423  2.1059396  0.0680372 0.9793602      NA
#> 3      f3     g3 -0.20450228 -2.4422318 -4.1314013 -0.1130397 0.9585258      NA
#> 4      f4     g4 -0.16305591 -0.3926680 -0.4118585 -0.1417504 1.0866933      NA
#> 5      f5     g5 -0.36569753  0.9718277 -1.9568060 -0.2670066 1.0888461      NA
#> 6      f6     g6 -0.21291378  3.6086383  1.7850510  0.0136696 1.0329443      NA
#>     p_value return_code
#> 1 0.3968035           0
#> 2 0.7553684           0
#> 3 0.6964196           0
#> 4 0.5399299           0
#> 5 0.1748733           0
#> 6 0.9346815           0

    The output is a data frame containing feature IDs, SNP IDs, effect estimates, and PP values for non-zero GxT interaction. Note that sigma_u, which represents estimates of random effect standard deviations, is NA, as we did not include random effects in this example.

    We emphasize that we set filter.geno to TRUE to exclude SNPs for which either of the homozygotes is missing in at least one of the control and treated conditions, which we call the “AA-AB” SNPs. We generally recommend excluding these SNPs, as it is often impossible to reliably estimate genotype effects with nonlinear regression. See our manuscript for further details. In practice, there is no need to pass this argument explicitly, as this is the default setting. Users can filter data differently before running map_qtl(), if preferred. In this case, filter.geno must be set to FALSE.

    Including random effects

    In this section, we demonstrate how to fit a nonlinear mixed effect model including polygenic or subject random effects.

    The following code generates an example kinship matrix. We use an identity matrix, which controls for the subject random effect, for simplicity. The row and column names of the matrix must correspond to the set of unique elements in the subject column in anno.

    kinship <- diag(n.sub)
rownames(kinship) <- colnames(kinship) <- unique(anno$subject)

    We then run map_qtl(), specifying the input arguments ranef and kinship. Note that the kinship argument can be omitted in practice when the matrix is identity.

    res.ranef <- map_qtl(
    candidate=d$candidate, fn="nonlinear",
    geno=d$geno, pheno=d$pheno, anno=anno,
    ranef=TRUE, kinship=kinship, filter.geno=TRUE)

head(res.ranef)
#>   feat_id snp_id       beta0     beta_g     beta_t    beta_gxt     sigma
#> 1      f1     g1 -0.08506785  0.9250610 -1.0026591  0.14887202 0.9069409
#> 2      f2     g2  0.15370807 -1.3154424  2.1059396  0.06803719 0.9793291
#> 3      f3     g3 -0.20450225 -2.4422319 -4.1314014 -0.11303948 0.9584988
#> 4      f4     g4 -0.16409346 -0.3912414 -0.4103359 -0.14390711 1.0497373
#> 5      f5     g5 -0.36531241  0.9714660 -1.9564032 -0.26743082 1.0642698
#> 6      f6     g6 -0.21291380  3.6086383  1.7850510  0.01366960 1.0329149
#>       sigma_u   p_value return_code
#> 1 0.168088458 0.3893308           0
#> 2 0.008207871 0.7553678           0
#> 3 0.007394676 0.6964162           0
#> 4 0.280995849 0.5230238           0
#> 5 0.230034960 0.1662258           0
#> 6 0.008340928 0.9346796           0

    The output now contains values in the sigma_u column, which represents estimates of random effect standard deviations.

    Session information 

    sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.4.3 (2025-02-28)
#> Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin20
#> Running under: macOS Sequoia 15.6.1
#> 
#> Matrix products: default
#> BLAS:   /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.4-arm64/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib 
#> LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.4-arm64/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;  LAPACK version 3.12.0
#> 
#> locale:
#> [1] en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8
#> 
#> time zone: America/New_York
#> tzcode source: internal
#> 
#> attached base packages:
#> [1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     
#> 
#> other attached packages:
#> [1] detectgxt_0.0.0.9000
#> 
#> loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
#>  [1] Matrix_1.7-4        jsonlite_2.0.0      dplyr_1.2.0        
#>  [4] compiler_4.4.3      tidyselect_1.2.1    jquerylib_0.1.4    
#>  [7] splines_4.4.3       systemfonts_1.3.1   textshaping_1.0.4  
#> [10] yaml_2.3.12         fastmap_1.2.0       lattice_0.22-9     
#> [13] R6_2.6.1            generics_0.1.4      knitr_1.51         
#> [16] BiocGenerics_0.52.0 htmlwidgets_1.6.4   tibble_3.3.1       
#> [19] desc_1.4.3          snpStats_1.56.0     bslib_0.10.0       
#> [22] pillar_1.11.1       rlang_1.1.7         cachem_1.1.0       
#> [25] xfun_0.56           fs_1.6.6            sass_0.4.10        
#> [28] otel_0.2.0          cli_3.6.5           withr_3.0.2        
#> [31] pkgdown_2.2.0       magrittr_2.0.4      zlibbioc_1.52.0    
#> [34] digest_0.6.39       grid_4.4.3          rstudioapi_0.18.0  
#> [37] mvtnorm_1.3-6       lifecycle_1.0.5     vctrs_0.7.2        
#> [40] evaluate_1.0.5      glue_1.8.0          ragg_1.5.0         
#> [43] survival_3.8-6      rmarkdown_2.30      purrr_1.2.1        
#> [46] tools_4.4.3         pkgconfig_2.0.3     htmltools_0.5.9

    References

    Alasoo, Kaur, Julia Rodrigues, Subhankar Mukhopadhyay, et al. 2018. “Shared Genetic Effects on Chromatin and Gene Expression Indicate a Role for Enhancer Priming in Immune Response.” Nature Genetics 50 (3): 424–31. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41588-018-0046-7.
    Matoba, Nana, Brandon D. Le, Jordan M. Valone, et al. 2024. “Stimulating Wnt Signaling Reveals Context-Dependent Genetic Effects on Gene Regulation in Primary Human Neural Progenitors.” Nature Neuroscience, ahead of print, September. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41593-024-01773-6.

    Developed by Yuriko Harigaya, Michael Love, William Valdar.

    Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
